    16	/// A basic dictionary that allows words to be added after instantiating.
    17	/// This is useful for user and file dictionaries that may change at runtime.
    18	///
    19	/// For immutable use-cases that require fuzzy lookups, such as the curated dictionary, prefer [`super::FstDictionary`],
    20	/// as it is much faster.
    21	///
    22	/// To combine the contents of multiple dictionaries, regardless of type, use
    23	/// [`super::MergedDictionary`].
    24	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Eq, PartialEq)]
    25	pub struct MutableDictionary {
    26	    /// All English words
    27	    word_map: WordMap,
    28	}
    29	
    30	/// The uncached function that is used to produce the original copy of the
    31	/// curated dictionary.
    32	fn uncached_inner_new() -> Arc<MutableDictionary> {
    33	    MutableDictionary::from_rune_files(
    34	        include_str!("../../dictionary.dict"),
    35	        include_str!("../../annotations.json"),
    36	    )
    37	    .map(Arc::new)
    38	    .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("Failed to load curated dictionary: {}", e))
    39	}
    40	
    41	static DICT: LazyLock<Arc<MutableDictionary>> = LazyLock::new(uncached_inner_new);
    42	
    43	impl MutableDictionary {
    44	    pub fn new() -> Self {
    45	        Self {
    46	            word_map: WordMap::default(),
    47	        }
    48	    }
    49	
    50	    pub fn from_rune_files(word_list: &str, attr_list: &str) -> Result<Self, rune::Error> {
    51	        let word_list = parse_word_list(word_list)?;
    52	        let attr_list = AttributeList::parse(attr_list)?;
    53	
    54	        // There will be at _least_ this number of words
    55	        let mut word_map = WordMap::default();
    56	
    57	        attr_list.expand_annotated_words(word_list, &mut word_map);
    58	
    59	        Ok(Self { word_map })
    60	    }
    61	
    62	    /// Create a dictionary from the curated dictionary included
    63	    /// in the Harper binary.
    64	    /// Consider using [`super::FstDictionary::curated()`] instead, as it is more performant for spellchecking.
    65	    pub fn curated() -> Arc<Self> {
    66	        (*DICT).clone()
    67	    }
    68	
    69	    /// Appends words to the dictionary.
    70	    /// It is significantly faster to append many words with one call than many
    71	    /// distinct calls to this function.
    72	    pub fn extend_words(
    73	        &mut self,
    74	        words: impl IntoIterator<Item = (impl AsRef<[char]>, DictWordMetadata)>,
    75	    ) {
    76	        for (chars, metadata) in words.into_iter() {
    77	            self.word_map.insert(WordMapEntry {
    78	                metadata,
    79	                canonical_spelling: chars.as_ref().into(),
    80	            })
    81	        }
    82	    }
    83	
    84	    /// Append a single word to the dictionary.
    85	    ///
    86	    /// If you are appending many words, consider using [`Self::extend_words`]
    87	    /// instead.
    88	    pub fn append_word(&mut self, word: impl AsRef<[char]>, metadata: DictWordMetadata) {
    89	        self.extend_words(std::iter::once((word.as_ref(), metadata)))
    90	    }
    91	
    92	    /// Append a single string to the dictionary.
    93	    ///
    94	    /// If you are appending many words, consider using [`Self::extend_words`]
    95	    /// instead.
    96	    pub fn append_word_str(&mut self, word: &str, metadata: DictWordMetadata) {
    97	        self.append_word(word.chars().collect::<Vec<_>>(), metadata)
    98	    }
    99	}
   100	
   101	impl Default for MutableDictionary {
   102	    fn default() -> Self {
   103	        Self::new()
   104	    }
   105	}
   106	
   107	impl Dictionary for MutableDictionary {
   108	    fn get_word_metadata(&self, word: &[char]) -> Option<Cow<'_, DictWordMetadata>> {
   109	        self.word_map
   110	            .get_with_chars(word)
   111	            .map(|v| Cow::Borrowed(&v.metadata))
   112	    }
   113	
   114	    fn contains_word(&self, word: &[char]) -> bool {
   115	        self.word_map.contains_chars(word)
   116	    }
   117	
   118	    fn contains_word_str(&self, word: &str) -> bool {
   119	        let chars: CharString = word.chars().collect();
   120	        self.contains_word(&chars)
   121	    }
   122	
   123	    fn get_word_metadata_str(&self, word: &str) -> Option<Cow<'_, DictWordMetadata>> {
   124	        let chars: CharString = word.chars().collect();
   125	        self.get_word_metadata(&chars)
   126	    }
   127	
   128	    fn get_correct_capitalization_of(&self, word: &[char]) -> Option<&'_ [char]> {
   129	        self.word_map
   130	            .get_with_chars(word)
   131	            .map(|v| v.canonical_spelling.as_slice())
   132	    }
   133	
   134	    /// Suggest a correct spelling for a given misspelled word.
   135	    /// `Self::word` is assumed to be quite small (n < 100).
   136	    /// `max_distance` relates to an optimization that allows the search
   137	    /// algorithm to prune large portions of the search.
   138	    fn fuzzy_match(
   139	        &'_ self,
   140	        word: &[char],
   141	        max_distance: u8,
   142	        max_results: usize,
   143	    ) -> Vec<FuzzyMatchResult<'_>> {
   144	        let misspelled_charslice = word.normalized();
   145	        let misspelled_charslice_lower = misspelled_charslice.to_lower();
   146	
   147	        let shortest_word_len = if misspelled_charslice.len() <= max_distance as usize {
   148	            1
   149	        } else {
   150	            misspelled_charslice.len() - max_distance as usize
   151	        };
   152	        let longest_word_len = misspelled_charslice.len() + max_distance as usize;
   153	
   154	        // Get candidate words
   155	        let words_to_search = self
   156	            .words_iter()
   157	            .filter(|word| (shortest_word_len..=longest_word_len).contains(&word.len()));
   158	
   159	        // Pre-allocated vectors for the edit-distance calculation
   160	        // 53 is the length of the longest word.
   161	        let mut buf_a = Vec::with_capacity(53);
   162	        let mut buf_b = Vec::with_capacity(53);
   163	
   164	        // Sort by edit-distance
   165	        words_to_search
   166	            .filter_map(|word| {
   167	                let dist =
   168	                    edit_distance_min_alloc(&misspelled_charslice, word, &mut buf_a, &mut buf_b);
   169	                let lowercase_dist = edit_distance_min_alloc(
   170	                    &misspelled_charslice_lower,
   171	                    word,
   172	                    &mut buf_a,
   173	                    &mut buf_b,
   174	                );
   175	
   176	                let smaller_dist = dist.min(lowercase_dist);
   177	                if smaller_dist <= max_distance {
   178	                    Some((word, smaller_dist))
   179	                } else {
   180	                    None
   181	                }
   182	            })
   183	            .sorted_unstable_by_key(|a| a.1)
   184	            .take(max_results)
   185	            .map(|(word, edit_distance)| FuzzyMatchResult {
   186	                word,
   187	                edit_distance,
   188	                metadata: self.get_word_metadata(word).unwrap(),
   189	            })
   190	            .collect()
   191	    }
   192	
   193	    fn fuzzy_match_str(
   194	        &'_ self,
   195	        word: &str,
   196	        max_distance: u8,
   197	        max_results: usize,
   198	    ) -> Vec<FuzzyMatchResult<'_>> {
   199	        let word: Vec<_> = word.chars().collect();
   200	        self.fuzzy_match(&word, max_distance, max_results)
   201	    }
   202	
   203	    fn words_iter(&self) -> Box<dyn Iterator<Item = &'_ [char]> + Send + '_> {
   204	        Box::new(
   205	            self.word_map
   206	                .iter()
   207	                .map(|v| v.canonical_spelling.as_slice()),
   208	        )
   209	    }
   210	
   211	    fn word_count(&self) -> usize {
   212	        self.word_map.len()
   213	    }
   214	
   215	    fn contains_exact_word(&self, word: &[char]) -> bool {
   216	        let normalized = word.normalized();
   217	
   218	        if let Some(found) = self.word_map.get_with_chars(normalized.as_ref())
   219	            && found.canonical_spelling.as_ref() == normalized.as_ref()
   220	        {
   221	            return true;
   222	        }
   223	
   224	        false
   225	    }
   226	
   227	    fn contains_exact_word_str(&self, word: &str) -> bool {
   228	        let word: CharString = word.chars().collect();
   229	        self.contains_exact_word(word.as_ref())
   230	    }
   231	
   232	    fn get_word_from_id(&self, id: &WordId) -> Option<&[char]> {
   233	        self.word_map.get(id).map(|w| w.canonical_spelling.as_ref())
   234	    }
   235	
   236	    fn find_words_with_prefix(&self, prefix: &[char]) -> Vec<Cow<'_, [char]>> {
   237	        let mut found = Vec::new();
   238	
   239	        for word in self.words_iter() {
   240	            if let Some(item_prefix) = word.get(0..prefix.len())
   241	                && item_prefix == prefix
   242	            {
   243	                found.push(Cow::Borrowed(word));
   244	            }
   245	        }
   246	
   247	        found
   248	    }
   249	
   250	    fn find_words_with_common_prefix(&self, word: &[char]) -> Vec<Cow<'_, [char]>> {
   251	        let mut found = Vec::new();
   252	
   253	        for item in self.words_iter() {
   254	            if let Some(item_prefix) = word.get(0..item.len())
   255	                && item_prefix == item
   256	            {
   257	                found.push(Cow::Borrowed(item));
   258	            }
   259	        }
   260	
   261	        found
   262	    }
   263	}
   264	
   265	impl From<MutableDictionary> for FstDictionary {
   266	    fn from(dict: MutableDictionary) -> Self {
   267	        let words = dict
   268	            .word_map
   269	            .into_iter()
   270	            .map(|entry| (entry.canonical_spelling, entry.metadata))
   271	            .collect();
   272	
   273	        FstDictionary::new(words)
   274	    }
   275	}
   276	
   277	#[cfg(test)]
   278	mod tests {
   279	    use std::borrow::Cow;
   280	
   281	    use hashbrown::HashSet;
   282	    use itertools::Itertools;
   283	
   284	    use crate::spell::{Dictionary, MutableDictionary};
   285	    use crate::{DictWordMetadata, char_string::char_string};
   286	
   287	    #[test]
   288	    fn curated_contains_no_duplicates() {
   289	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   290	        assert!(dict.words_iter().all_unique());
   291	    }
   292	
   293	    #[test]
   294	    fn curated_matches_capitalized() {
   295	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   296	        assert!(dict.contains_word_str("this"));
   297	        assert!(dict.contains_word_str("This"));
   298	    }
   299	
   300	    // "This" is a determiner when used similarly to "the"
   301	    // but when used alone it's a "demonstrative pronoun".
   302	    // Harper previously wrongly classified it as a noun.
   303	    #[test]
   304	    fn this_is_determiner() {
   305	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   306	        assert!(dict.get_word_metadata_str("this").unwrap().is_determiner());
   307	        assert!(dict.get_word_metadata_str("This").unwrap().is_determiner());
   308	    }
   309	
   310	    #[test]
   311	    fn several_is_quantifier() {
   312	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   313	        assert!(
   314	            dict.get_word_metadata_str("several")
   315	                .unwrap()
   316	                .is_quantifier()
   317	        );
   318	    }
   319	
   320	    #[test]
   321	    fn few_is_quantifier() {
   322	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   323	        assert!(dict.get_word_metadata_str("few").unwrap().is_quantifier());
   324	    }
   325	
   326	    #[test]
   327	    fn fewer_is_quantifier() {
   328	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   329	        assert!(dict.get_word_metadata_str("fewer").unwrap().is_quantifier());
   330	    }
   331	
   332	    #[test]
   333	    fn than_is_conjunction() {
   334	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   335	        assert!(dict.get_word_metadata_str("than").unwrap().is_conjunction());
   336	        assert!(dict.get_word_metadata_str("Than").unwrap().is_conjunction());
   337	    }

   343	        assert!(dict.get_word_metadata_str("Herself").unwrap().is_pronoun());
   344	    }

   350	    }

   356	    }

   358	    #[test]
   359	    fn fuzzy_result_sorted_by_edit_distance() {
   360	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   361	
   362	        let results = dict.fuzzy_match_str("hello", 3, 100);
   363	        let is_sorted_by_dist = results
   364	            .iter()
   365	            .map(|fm| fm.edit_distance)
   366	            .tuple_windows()
   367	            .all(|(a, b)| a <= b);
   368	
   369	        assert!(is_sorted_by_dist)
   370	    }
   371	
   372	    #[test]
   373	    fn there_is_not_a_pronoun() {
   374	        let dict = MutableDictionary::curated();
   375	
   376	        assert!(!dict.get_word_metadata_str("there").unwrap().is_nominal());
   377	        assert!(!dict.get_word_metadata_str("there").unwrap().is_pronoun());
   378	    }

   461	    fn gets_prefixes_as_expected() {
   462	        let mut dict = MutableDictionary::new();
   463	        dict.append_word_str("predict", DictWordMetadata::default());
   464	        dict.append_word_str("prelude", DictWordMetadata::default());
   465	        dict.append_word_str("preview", DictWordMetadata::default());
   466	        dict.append_word_str("dwight", DictWordMetadata::default());
   467	
   468	        let with_prefix = dict.find_words_with_prefix(char_string!("pre").as_slice());
   469	
   470	        assert_eq!(with_prefix.len(), 3);
   471	        assert!(with_prefix.contains(&Cow::Owned(char_string!("predict").into_vec())));
   472	        assert!(with_prefix.contains(&Cow::Owned(char_string!("prelude").into_vec())));
   473	        assert!(with_prefix.contains(&Cow::Owned(char_string!("preview").into_vec())));
   474	    }
   475	
   476	    #[test]
   477	    fn gets_common_prefixes_as_expected() {
   478	        let mut dict = MutableDictionary::new();
   479	        dict.append_word_str("pre", DictWordMetadata::default());
   480	        dict.append_word_str("prep", DictWordMetadata::default());
   481	        dict.append_word_str("dwight", DictWordMetadata::default());
   482	
   483	        let with_prefix =
   484	            dict.find_words_with_common_prefix(char_string!("preposition").as_slice());
   485	
   486	        assert_eq!(with_prefix.len(), 2);
   487	        assert!(with_prefix.contains(&Cow::Owned(char_string!("pre").into_vec())));
   488	        assert!(with_prefix.contains(&Cow::Owned(char_string!("prep").into_vec())));
   489	    }
   490	}

   202	            })
   203	            .to_string(),
   204	        )
   205	        .unwrap();
   206	
   207	        let mut expanded = WordMap::default();
   208	
   209	        attributes.expand_annotated_words(words, &mut expanded);
   210	
   211	        let giant_data = expanded.get_with_str("giant").unwrap();
   212	        assert!(giant_data.metadata.is_noun());
   213	
   214	        let giants_data = expanded.get_with_str("giants").unwrap();
   215	        assert!(giants_data.metadata.is_plural_noun());
   216	    }
   217	}

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /testbed/harper-core/src/spell/rune/word_list.rs:
     1	use super::Error;
     2	use crate::CharString;
     3	
     4	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
     5	pub struct AnnotatedWord {
     6	    pub letters: CharString,
     7	    pub annotations: Vec<char>,
     8	}
     9	
    10	/// Parse a Rune word list
    11	///
    12	/// Returns [`None`] if the given string is invalid.
    13	pub fn parse_word_list(source: &str) -> Result<Vec<AnnotatedWord>, Error> {
    14	    let mut lines = source.lines();
    15	
    16	    let approx_item_count = lines
    17	        .next()
    18	        .ok_or(Error::MalformedItemCount)?
    19	        .parse()
    20	        .map_err(|_| Error::MalformedItemCount)?;
    21	
    22	    let mut words = Vec::with_capacity(approx_item_count);
    23	
    24	    for line in lines {
    25	        // Ignore blank lines and full line comments.
    26	        if line.is_empty() || line.starts_with('#') {
    27	            continue;
    28	        }
    29	
    30	        let entry: &str;
    31	        if let Some((entry_part, _comment_part)) = line.split_once('#') {
    32	            entry = entry_part.trim_end();
    33	        } else {
    34	            entry = line.trim_end();
    35	        }
    36	
    37	        let word: &str;
    38	        let attr: Option<&str>;
    39	        if let Some((word_part, attr_part)) = entry.split_once('/') {
    40	            word = word_part;
    41	            attr = Some(attr_part);
    42	        } else {
    43	            word = entry;
    44	            attr = None;
    45	        }
    46	
    47	        words.push(AnnotatedWord {
    48	            letters: word.chars().collect(),
    49	            annotations: attr.unwrap_or_default().chars().collect(),
    50	        })
    51	    }
    52	
    53	    Ok(words)
    54	}
    55	
    56	#[cfg(test)]
    57	mod tests {
    58	    use super::super::tests::TEST_WORD_LIST;
    59	    use super::parse_word_list;
    60	
    61	    #[test]
    62	    fn can_parse_test_file() {
    63	        let list = parse_word_list(TEST_WORD_LIST).unwrap();
    64	
    65	        assert_eq!(list.last().unwrap().annotations.len(), 0);
    66	        assert_eq!(list.len(), 4);
    67	    }
    68	}

     1	use hashbrown::HashMap;
     2	use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
     3	use smallvec::ToSmallVec;
     4	
     5	use super::super::word_map::{WordMap, WordMapEntry};
     6	use super::Error;
     7	use super::affix_replacement::AffixReplacement;
     8	use super::expansion::Property;
     9	use super::expansion::{
    10	    AffixEntryKind,
    11	    AffixEntryKind::{Prefix, Suffix},
    12	    Expansion, HumanReadableExpansion,
    13	};
    14	use super::word_list::AnnotatedWord;
    15	use crate::dict_word_metadata_orthography::OrthFlags;
    16	use crate::spell::WordId;
    17	use crate::{CharString, DictWordMetadata, Span};
    18	
    19	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
    20	pub struct AttributeList {
    21	    /// Key = Affix Flag
    22	    affixes: HashMap<char, Expansion>,
    23	    properties: HashMap<char, Property>,
    24	}
    25	
    26	impl AttributeList {
    27	    fn into_human_readable(self) -> HumanReadableAttributeList {
    28	        HumanReadableAttributeList {
    29	            affixes: self
    30	                .affixes
    31	                .into_iter()
    32	                .map(|(affix, exp)| (affix, exp.into_human_readable()))
    33	                .collect(),
    34	            properties: self.properties,
    35	        }
    36	    }
    37	
    38	    pub fn parse(source: &str) -> Result<Self, Error> {
    39	        let human_readable: Result<HumanReadableAttributeList, _> = serde_json::from_str(source);
    40	        human_readable
    41	            .map_err(Error::from)
    42	            .and_then(|parsed| parsed.into_normal())
    43	    }
    44	
    45	    /// Expand an [`AnnotatedWord`] into a list of full words, including itself.
    46	    ///
    47	    /// This function processes a word and its attributes to:
    48	    /// 1. Apply properties to the base word
    49	    /// 2. Generate derived words using affix rules
    50	    /// 3. Handle conditional expansions
    51	    /// 4. Manage cross-product expansions
    52	    ///
    53	    /// # Arguments
    54	    /// * `word` - The word to expand, along with its attributes
    55	    /// * `dest` - The WordMap to store the expanded words and their metadata
    56	    pub fn expand_annotated_word(&self, annotated_word: AnnotatedWord, word_map: &mut WordMap) {
    57	        // Pre-allocate space in the destination map for better performance
    58	        word_map.reserve(annotated_word.annotations.len() + 1);
    59	
    60	        // Initialize base metadata that will be applied to all derived forms
    61	        let mut base_metadata = DictWordMetadata::default();
    62	
    63	        // Store metadata that should only be applied if certain conditions are met
    64	        let orth_flags = OrthFlags::from_letters(&annotated_word.letters);
    65	        base_metadata.orth_info = orth_flags;
    66	
    67	        let mut conditional_expansion_metadata = Vec::new();
    68	
    69	        // First pass: Process all properties to build the base metadata
    70	        // Properties directly modify the word's metadata (e.g., part of speech, usage)
    71	        for attr in &annotated_word.annotations {
    72	            let Some(property) = self.properties.get(attr) else {
    73	                continue;
    74	            };
    75	            base_metadata.merge(&property.metadata);
    76	        }
    77	
    78	        // Second pass: Process all affix rules to generate derived forms
    79	        for attr in &annotated_word.annotations {
    80	            // Skip if this attribute isn't an affix rule
    81	            let Some(expansion) = self.affixes.get(attr) else {
    82	                continue;
    83	            };
    84	
    85	            // Add any base metadata from this affix rule
    86	            base_metadata.merge(&expansion.base_metadata);
    87	
    88	            // Track new words generated by this affix rule
    89	            let mut new_words: HashMap<CharString, DictWordMetadata> = HashMap::new();
    90	
    91	            // Apply each replacement rule in this affix
    92	            for replacement in &expansion.replacements {
    93	                if let Some(replaced) =
    94	                    Self::apply_replacement(replacement, &annotated_word.letters, expansion.kind)
    95	                {
    96	                    // Get or create metadata for this new word form
    97	                    let metadata = new_words.entry(replaced.clone()).or_default();
    98	
    99	                    // Process each target for this replacement
   100	                    for target in &expansion.target {
   101	                        if let Some(condition) = &target.if_base {
   102	                            // Store conditional metadata to be applied later
   103	                            conditional_expansion_metadata.push((
   104	                                replaced.clone(),
   105	                                target.metadata.clone(),
   106	                                condition.clone(),
   107	                            ));
   108	                        } else {
   109	                            // Apply target metadata immediately
   110	                            metadata.merge(&target.metadata);
   111	                        }
   112	                    }
   113	                }
   114	            }
   115	
   116	            // Handle cross-product expansions (e.g., both prefix and suffix)
   117	            if expansion.cross_product {
   118	                // Collect attributes that should be applied to the opposite affix type
   119	                let mut opposite_attributes = Vec::new();
   120	
   121	                // Add properties that should propagate to derived forms
   122	                for attr in &annotated_word.annotations {
   123	                    let Some(property) = self.properties.get(attr) else {
   124	                        continue;
   125	                    };
   126	                    if expansion.kind == Prefix || property.propagate {
   127	                        opposite_attributes.push(*attr);
   128	                    }
   129	                }
   130	
   131	                // Add affix attributes of the opposite type
   132	                for attr in &annotated_word.annotations {
   133	                    let Some(attr_def) = self.affixes.get(attr) else {
   134	                        continue;
   135	                    };
   136	                    // This checks if the current affix is of the opposite type
   137	                    if (attr_def.kind != Prefix) != (expansion.kind != Prefix) {
   138	                        opposite_attributes.push(*attr);
   139	                    }
   140	                }
   141	
   142	                // Recursively process each new word form
   143	                for (new_word, metadata) in new_words {
   144	                    self.expand_annotated_word(
   145	                        AnnotatedWord {
   146	                            letters: new_word.clone(),
   147	                            annotations: opposite_attributes.clone(),
   148	                        },
   149	                        word_map,
   150	                    );
   151	                    // Update the metadata of the expanded word
   152	                    let target_metadata = word_map.get_metadata_mut_chars(&new_word).unwrap();
   153	                    target_metadata.merge(&metadata);
   154	                    target_metadata.derived_from =
   155	                        Some(WordId::from_word_chars(&annotated_word.letters));
   156	                }
   157	            } else {
   158	                // Simple case: no cross-product expansion needed
   159	                for (key, mut value) in new_words.into_iter() {
   160	                    value.derived_from = Some(WordId::from_word_chars(&annotated_word.letters));
   161	
   162	                    if let Some(existing_metadata) = word_map.get_metadata_mut_chars(&key) {
   163	                        // Merge with existing metadata
   164	                        existing_metadata.merge(&value);
   165	                    } else {
   166	                        // Add new entry
   167	                        word_map.insert(WordMapEntry {
   168	                            canonical_spelling: key,
   169	                            metadata: value,
   170	                        });
   171	                    }
   172	                }
   173	            }
   174	        }
   175	
   176	        // Finalize the metadata for the base word
   177	        let mut full_metadata = base_metadata;
   178	
   179	        // Merge with any existing metadata for this word
   180	        if let Some(existing_metadata) = word_map.get_with_chars(&annotated_word.letters) {
   181	            full_metadata.merge(&existing_metadata.metadata);
   182	        }
   183	
   184	        // Store the final metadata for the base word
   185	        word_map.insert(WordMapEntry {
   186	            metadata: full_metadata.clone(),
   187	            canonical_spelling: annotated_word.letters,
   188	        });
   189	
   190	        // Process any conditional expansions
   191	        for (letters, metadata, condition) in conditional_expansion_metadata {
   192	            // Check if the condition is satisfied by the base word's metadata
   193	            let condition_satisfied = full_metadata.or(&condition) == full_metadata;
   194	            if !condition_satisfied {
   195	                continue;
   196	            }
   197	
   198	            // Apply the conditional metadata
   199	            word_map
   200	                .get_metadata_mut_chars(&letters)
   201	                .unwrap()
   202	                .merge(&metadata);
   203	        }
   204	    }
   205	
   206	    /// Expand an iterator of annotated words into strings.
   207	    /// Note that this does __not__ guarantee that produced words will be
   208	    /// unique.
   209	    pub fn expand_annotated_words(
   210	        &self,
   211	        words: impl IntoIterator<Item = AnnotatedWord>,
   212	        dest: &mut WordMap,
   213	    ) {
   214	        for word in words {
   215	            self.expand_annotated_word(word, dest);
   216	        }
   217	    }
   218	
   219	    fn apply_replacement(
   220	        replacement: &AffixReplacement,
   221	        letters: &[char],
   222	        kind: AffixEntryKind,
   223	    ) -> Option<CharString> {
   224	        if replacement.condition.len() > letters.len() {
   225	            return None;
   226	        }
   227	
   228	        let target_span = if kind == Suffix {
   229	            Span::new(letters.len() - replacement.condition.len(), letters.len())
   230	        } else {
   231	            Span::new(0, replacement.condition.len())
   232	        };
   233	
   234	        let target_segment = target_span.get_content(letters);
   235	
   236	        if replacement.condition.matches(target_segment) {
   237	            let mut replaced_segment = letters.to_smallvec();
   238	            let mut remove: CharString = replacement.remove.to_smallvec();
   239	
   240	            if kind != Suffix {
   241	                replaced_segment.reverse();
   242	            } else {
   243	                remove.reverse();
   244	            }
   245	
   246	            for c in &remove {
   247	                let last = replaced_segment.last()?;
   248	
   249	                if last == c {
   250	                    replaced_segment.pop();
   251	                } else {
   252	                    return None;
   253	                }
   254	            }
   255	
   256	            let mut to_add = replacement.add.to_vec();
   257	
   258	            if kind != Suffix {
   259	                to_add.reverse()
   260	            }
   261	
   262	            replaced_segment.extend(to_add);
   263	
   264	            if kind != Suffix {
   265	                replaced_segment.reverse();
   266	            }
   267	
   268	            return Some(replaced_segment);
   269	        }
   270	
   271	        None
   272	    }
   273	}
   274	
   275	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   276	pub struct HumanReadableAttributeList {
   277	    affixes: HashMap<char, HumanReadableExpansion>,
   278	    properties: HashMap<char, Property>,
   279	}
   280	
   281	impl HumanReadableAttributeList {
   282	    pub fn into_normal(self) -> Result<AttributeList, Error> {
   283	        let mut affixes = HashMap::with_capacity(self.affixes.len());
   284	
   285	        for (affix, expansion) in self.affixes.into_iter() {
   286	            affixes.insert(affix, expansion.into_normal()?);
   287	        }
   288	
   289	        Ok(AttributeList {
   290	            affixes,
   291	            properties: self.properties,
   292	        })
   293	    }
   294	}

So:
- `verse/~NgSVGdrWnX` - verse: noun (N), verb (V), etc.
- `versed/~JVtTU` - versed: adjective (J), verb (V)
- `versus/~PV` - versus: preposition (P), verb (V)

Wait, `versus` has `V` (verb) flag? That seems wrong. And `verse` also has `V` flag. The problem described in the PR is that people use "verse" as a verb (meaning "versus"), and the dictionary currently allows this because "verse" is marked as a verb.

     1	use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
     2	
     3	use super::Error;
     4	use super::affix_replacement::{AffixReplacement, HumanReadableAffixReplacement};
     5	use crate::DictWordMetadata;
     6	
     7	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Serialize, Deserialize, PartialEq)]
     8	#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
     9	pub enum AffixEntryKind {
    10	    Suffix,
    11	    Prefix,
    12	}
    13	
    14	/// Defines how a word can be transformed and what metadata to apply
    15	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
    16	pub struct Expansion {
    17	    /// Whether this is a prefix or suffix expansion
    18	    pub kind: AffixEntryKind,
    19	    /// If true, allows this expansion to be combined with others (e.g., both prefix and suffix)
    20	    pub cross_product: bool,
    21	    /// The replacement rules that define how to modify the word
    22	    pub replacements: Vec<AffixReplacement>,
    23	    /// Metadata to apply to the transformed word
    24	    pub target: Vec<MetadataExpansion>,
    25	    /// Metadata to apply to the base word when this expansion is applied
    26	    pub base_metadata: DictWordMetadata,
    27	}
    28	
    29	impl Expansion {
    30	    pub fn into_human_readable(self) -> HumanReadableExpansion {
    31	        HumanReadableExpansion {
    32	            kind: self.kind,
    33	            cross_product: self.cross_product,
    34	            replacements: self
    35	                .replacements
    36	                .iter()
    37	                .map(AffixReplacement::to_human_readable)
    38	                .collect(),
    39	            target: self.target,
    40	            base_metadata: self.base_metadata,
    41	        }
    42	    }
    43	}
    44	
    45	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    46	pub struct MetadataExpansion {
    47	    pub metadata: DictWordMetadata,
    48	    pub if_base: Option<DictWordMetadata>,
    49	}
    50	
    51	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    52	pub struct HumanReadableExpansion {
    53	    pub kind: AffixEntryKind,
    54	    pub cross_product: bool,
    55	    pub replacements: Vec<HumanReadableAffixReplacement>,
    56	    pub target: Vec<MetadataExpansion>,
    57	    pub base_metadata: DictWordMetadata,
    58	}
    59	
    60	impl HumanReadableExpansion {
    61	    pub fn into_normal(self) -> Result<Expansion, Error> {
    62	        let mut replacements = Vec::with_capacity(self.replacements.len());
    63	
    64	        for replacement in &self.replacements {
    65	            replacements.push(replacement.to_normal()?);
    66	        }
    67	
    68	        Ok(Expansion {
    69	            kind: self.kind,
    70	            cross_product: self.cross_product,
    71	            replacements,
    72	            target: self.target,
    73	            base_metadata: self.base_metadata,
    74	        })
    75	    }
    76	}
    77	
    78	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    79	pub struct Property {
    80	    /// Whether the metadata will propagate to all derived words.
    81	    #[serde(default)]
    82	    pub propagate: bool,
    83	    /// The metadata applied to the word.
    84	    pub metadata: DictWordMetadata,
    85	}
    86

2026-06-01 04:13:16,828 - INFO - swea-agent-Automattic__harper-2973 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I understand the dictionary format. Let's decode the entry for `verse`:

`verse/~NgSVGdrWnX`:
- `~` = common word
- `N` = noun
- `g` = possessive suffix (`'s`) -> can expand to `verse's` 
- `S` = plural suffix -> can expand to `verses`
- `V` = verb
- `G` = `-ing` suffix -> `versing`
- `d` = `-ed` suffix -> `versed`
- `r` = `re-` prefix -> `reverse`
- `W` = `con-` prefix -> `converse`
- `n` = nominalization
- `X` = plural of nominalization

So `verse` is marked as both a noun AND a verb. Since it's marked as a verb, it can expand to:
- `verse` (verb lemma)
- `verses` (third person singular)
- `versing` (progressive)
- `versed` (past)

And as a noun:
- `verse` (noun)
- `verses` (plural noun)
- `verse's` (possessive)

The problem is that the verb forms of "verse" (meaning to write poetry) are being confused with the misuse of "verse" meaning "versus". But actually, "verse" IS a legitimate verb meaning "to make verse" or "to tell in verse", though it's very rare.

The PR description says:
- "versing" is archaic - we should probably remove it from the dictionary
- "verses" may be used as a verb form and as a misspelling for the preposition

So the solution seems to be about removing or restricting the verb form of "verse". Let me look at what changes are needed.

Looking at the dictionary entry:
```
verse/~NgSVGdrWnX
```

The `V` flag makes it a verb. If we remove `V`, then `G`, `d`, and `S` won't generate verb forms. But wait, `S` is also used for plural nouns. 

Actually, looking more carefully at the annotations system: the `S` flag is the plural suffix, and it generates both noun plurals (if the base is a noun) and third-person singular verbs (if the base is a verb). The `d` flag is the past tense suffix, and it generates adjectives and past tense verbs. The `G` flag generates progressive verbs.

The issue is complex. The PR description says we need context to disambiguate. But the simplest first step would be to remove "versing" from the dictionary since it's described as archaic.

Let me search for tests related to "verse" to understand what's expected:

     9	pub fn lint_group() -> LintGroup {

    11	
    12	    // Each correction pair has a single bad form and a single correct form.

    28	                );

The phrase_set_corrections module is where 1-to-1 and many-to-many phrase mappings are defined. This seems like a good place to add the "verse" as a verb corrections.\n\nBut wait, let me reconsider the problem. The PR description says:\n1. "verse" used as a verb (meaning "versus") should be flagged\n2. "versing" is archaic - we can probably remove it from the dictionary\n3. "verses" may be used as a verb form and as a misspelling for the preposition\n\nThe solution likely involves:\n1. Modifying the dictionary entry for `verse` to remove the verb (V) flag, so it\'s only a noun\n2. Or adding a new lint rule that catches "verse" used as a verb\n\nBut actually, "verse" IS a legitimate (though rare) verb meaning "to compose verse" or "to tell in verse". So we can\'t just remove the verb flag entirely. The PR description says we need context to disambiguate.\n\nHowever, looking at the PR description more carefully, it says:\n- "versing" is archaic - we can probably remove it from the dictionary\n- The suggested replacements are "face", "challenge", "play against", "compete against"\n\nThis suggests that the primary goal is to flag when "verse" is used as a synonym for "versus" (i.e., to compete against). So the approach might be:\n1. Remove "versing" from the dictionary (archaic)\n2. 2026-06-01 04:14:25,439 - INFO - swea-agent-Automattic__harper-2973 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I understand the structure. The phrase_set_corrections module is where 1-to-1 and many-to-many phrase mappings are defined. This seems like a good place to add the "verse" as a verb corrections.

But wait, let me reconsider the problem. The PR description says:
1. "verse" used as a verb (meaning "versus") should be flagged
2. "versing" is archaic - we can probably remove it from the dictionary
3. "verses" may be used as a verb form and as a misspelling for the preposition

The solution likely involves:
1. Modifying the dictionary entry for `verse` to remove the verb (V) flag, so it's only a noun
2. Or adding a new lint rule that catches "verse" used as a verb

But actually, "verse" IS a legitimate (though rare) verb meaning "to compose verse" or "to tell in verse". So we can't just remove the verb flag entirely. The PR description says we need context to disambiguate.

However, looking at the PR description more carefully, it says:
- "versing" is archaic - we can probably remove it from the dictionary
- The suggested replacements are "face", "challenge", "play against", "compete against"

This suggests that the primary goal is to flag when "verse" is used as a synonym for "versus" (i.e., to compete against). So the approach might be:
1. Remove "versing" from the dictionary (archaic)
2. Add a lint rule that catches "verse", "verses", "versed", "versing" when used as verb forms meaning "compete against"

But looking at the structure of the project, the simplest approach that fits the existing patterns would be to modify the dictionary entry for `verse` and related words.

Let me check what the `~` prefix does for the entry. The `~` means "common". So `verse/~NgSVGdrWnX` means:
- common word
- noun (N)
- possessive (g)
- plural (S)
- verb (V)
- progressive/gerund (G)
- past (d)
- re- prefix (r)
- con- prefix (W)
- nominalization (n)
- plural nominalization (X)

If we remove `V`, then `G` and `d` won't generate verb forms (but they might still generate adjective forms). Actually wait, `G` generates both verb progressive and noun/adjective forms. `d` generates both adjective and past verb forms.

Let me think about this differently. Let me check what the actual approach should be based on similar existing lints. Looking at `CompulseToCompel` in phrase_set_corrections, it maps the archaic verb "compulse" to "compel". Similarly, "versing" is described as archaic.

So the approach might be to add entries in phrase_set_corrections like:
- "versing" → "facing" (or "playing against")
- "versed" → "faced" (when used as verb)
- "verses" → "faces" (when used as verb)

But that would be overly aggressive since "versed" and "verses" have legitimate uses.

Let me look at the PR description again more carefully. It says:
- "versed" also passes spellcheck because it's also a different word, most commonly seen in the phrase "well-versed"
- "verse", used as the lemma is of course also a word, a singular noun
- "versing" is archaic. We can probably remove it from the dictionary.
- "verses" may be used as a verb form and as a misspelling for the preposition

So the key actionable items seem to be:
1. Remove "versing" from the dictionary (since it's archaic)
2. Maybe add a rule for "verses" as a misspelling of "versus"

But actually, "versing" is already in the dictionary via the `G` suffix on `verse`. So if we remove the verb flag from `verse`, then `versing` won't be generated as a verb form.

Wait, let me re-examine. The `G` flag generates `-ing` forms. If `verse` is a verb (V), then `G` generates `versing` as a progressive verb. If `verse` is NOT a verb, `G` would still generate `versing` but it would be tagged as noun/adjective (gerund). 

Actually, looking at the `G` expansion in annotations.json:
```json
"G": {
    "#": "'-ing' suffix",
    ...
    "target": [
        {
            "metadata": {
                "verb": {
                    "verb_form": "PROGRESSIVE"
                },
                "noun": {
                    "is_mass": true
                },
                "adjective": {}
            }
        }
    ],
    "base_metadata": {}
}
```

The `G` suffix always adds verb, noun, and adjective metadata regardless of the base. So even without `V` on the base, `versing` would still be generated with verb metadata.

Similarly for `d`:
```json
"d": {
    "#": "'-(e)d' suffix; verb past",
    ...
    "target": [
        {
            "metadata": {
                "adjective": {
                    "degree": "Positive"
                }
            }
        },
        {
            "if_base": {
                "verb": {}
            },
            "metadata": {
                "verb": {
                    "verb_form": "PAST"
                }
            }
        }
    ],
    "base_metadata": {}
}
```

The `d` suffix adds adjective always, but only adds verb past if the base is a verb. So if we remove `V` from `verse`, `versed` won't be tagged as a verb past form.

And for `S`:
```json
"S": {
    ...
    "target": [
        {
            "if_base": {
                "noun": {}
            },
            "metadata": {
                "noun": {
                    "is_plural": true
                }
            }
        },
        {
            "if_base": {
                "verb": {}
            },
            "metadata": {
                "verb": {
                    "verb_form": "THIRD_PERSON_SINGULAR"
                }
            }
        }
    ],
    "base_metadata": {}
}
```

If we remove `V` from `verse`, `verses` won't be tagged as a third-person singular verb.

So the key change is: **remove the `V` flag from `verse` in the dictionary**. This would mean:
- `verse` is no longer a verb lemma
- `verses` is no longer a third-person singular verb (only plural noun)
- `versed` is no longer a past tense verb (only adjective, as in "well-versed")
- `versing` would still be generated (since `G` doesn't check if base is verb) but... actually let me check if `G` requires the base to have certain metadata.

Looking at the `G` expansion target, it doesn't have `if_base`, so it always applies. So `versing` would still be generated even without `V`. 

But the PR says "versing" is archaic and we should probably remove it from the dictionary. To prevent `versing` from being generated, we would need to remove the `G` flag from `verse` as well.

But wait, "versing" as a gerund/noun ("the versing of the poem") might be legitimate, though rare. The PR says it's archaic.

Let me think about what changes need to be made based on the PR description and the codebase understanding:

1. The dictionary entry for `verse` currently is `verse/~NgSVGdrWnX`. This includes `V` (verb). The verb sense of "verse" is legitimate but rare ("to compose verse"). However, the PR is about flagging the misuse of "verse" as a verb meaning "to compete against" (from "versus").

2. "versing" is archaic. The PR says "We can probably remove it from the dictionary."

3. The dictionary entry for `versed` is `versed/~JVtTU` - already marked as adjective (J) and verb (V, t=preterite, T=past participle, U=un- prefix). The adjective sense ("well-versed") is legitimate.

4. The dictionary entry for `versus` is `versus/~PV` - preposition (P) and verb (V). Wait, `versus` has `V`? That seems wrong - "versus" is a preposition, not a verb. But maybe `V` was left there intentionally or by mistake.

Let me check if `versus` being a verb is causing issues. Actually, `versus` as a verb entry means the system might accept "versused", "versusing" etc. which are definitely wrong.

Let me now focus on what changes to make:

1. **Remove `V` from `versus`**: `versus` should only be a preposition (`P`), not a verb. Currently: `versus/~PV` → should be `versus/~P`

2. **Handle "verse" as verb**: The `V` flag on `verse` allows its use as a verb. "Verse" is a legitimate but very rare verb meaning "to compose verse". However, most uses of "verse" as a verb are actually misuses of "versus". Since context is needed to disambiguate, we might need to:
   - Keep `V` on `verse` for the legitimate verb sense
   - Add a lint rule for the misuse

   OR
   
   - Remove `V` from `verse` since the verb sense is so rare and the misuse is common

3. **Remove "versing"**: The PR says it's archaic. Since `G` suffix generates "versing" regardless of `V`, we need to remove `G` from `verse`.

     1	use std::fmt::Display;
     2	
     3	use is_macro::Is;
     4	use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
     5	
     6	/// The general category a [`Lint`](super::Lint) falls into.
     7	/// There's no reason not to add a new item here if you are adding a new rule that doesn't fit
     8	/// the existing categories.
     9	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Serialize, Deserialize, Is, Default, Hash, PartialEq, Eq)]
    10	pub enum LintKind {
    11	    Agreement,
    12	    /// For errors where words are joined or split at the wrong boundaries
    13	    /// (e.g., "each and everyone" vs. "each and every one")
    14	    BoundaryError,
    15	    Capitalization,
    16	    /// For cases where a word or phrase is misused for a similar-sounding word or phrase,
    17	    /// where the incorrect version makes logical sense (e.g., 'egg corn' for 'acorn',
    18	    /// 'on mass' for 'en masse').
    19	    Eggcorn,
    20	    /// For suggesting improvements that enhance clarity or impact without fixing errors
    21	    Enhancement,
    22	    Formatting,
    23	    Grammar,
    24	    /// For cases where a word is mistakenly used for a similar-sounding word with a different meaning
    25	    /// (e.g., 'eluded to' instead of 'alluded to'). Unlike eggcorns, these don't create new meanings.
    26	    Malapropism,
    27	    /// For any other lint that doesn't fit neatly into the other categories
    28	    #[default]
    29	    Miscellaneous,
    30	    Nonstandard,
    31	    /// For issues with punctuation, including hyphenation in compound adjectives
    32	    /// (e.g., "face first" vs. "face-first" when used before a noun)
    33	    Punctuation,
    34	    Readability,
    35	    /// For cases where words duplicate meaning that's already expressed
    36	    /// (e.g., "basic fundamentals" → "fundamentals", "free gift" → "gift")
    37	    Redundancy,
    38	    /// For variations that are standard in some regions or dialects but not others
    39	    Regionalism,
    40	    Repetition,
    41	    /// When your brain doesn't know the right spelling.
    42	    /// This should only be used by linters doing spellcheck on individual words.
    43	    Spelling,
    44	    /// For cases where multiple options are correct but one is preferred for style or clarity,
    45	    /// such as expanding abbreviations in formal writing (e.g., 'min' → 'minimum')
    46	    Style,
    47	    /// When your brain knows the right spelling but your fingers made a mistake.
    48	    /// (e.g., 'can be seem' → 'can be seen')
    49	    Typo,
    50	    /// For conventional word usage and standard collocations
    51	    /// (e.g., 'by accident' vs. 'on accident' in standard English)
    52	    Usage,
    53	    /// For choosing between different words or phrases in a given context
    54	    WordChoice,
    55	}

2026-06-01 04:17:40,759 - INFO - swea-agent-Automattic__harper-2973 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I have a thorough understanding. Let me figure out the exact changes needed.

Based on the PR description, the issue is about "verse" being used as a verb related to "versus". The approach should be:

1. **Remove the verb (`V`) flag from `verse`** in the dictionary - because "verse" as a verb meaning "to compete against" is nonstandard, and the legitimate verb sense "to compose verse" is extremely rare and not worth the false negatives.

2. **Remove `V` from `versus`** - "versus" is a preposition, not a verb.

3. **Remove `G` from `verse`** - to prevent "versing" from being generated, since it's archaic.

But wait - I need to be more careful. Let me check: does removing `V` from `verse` affect anything else? The `r` (re- prefix) and `W` (con- prefix) expansions would still work. `n` (nominalization) and `X` (plural nominalization) would still work. `S` (plural/third-person) would still generate plural noun. `d` would still generate adjective. `g` (possessive) still works.

Actually, `G` (the -ing suffix) has no `if_base` condition, so it would still generate "versing" as a noun/adjective even without `V`. To truly remove "versing", we'd need to remove `G` from `verse`.

But "versing" as a noun (meaning "the act of composing verse") is legitimate, though archaic. The PR says to remove it.

Let me check what happens when we remove `V` and `G` from `verse`:

Current: `verse/~NgSVGdrWnX`
Proposed: `verse/~NgSdrWnX`

This would:
- Keep noun (N), possessive (g), plural (S), past/adjective (d), re- prefix (r), con- prefix (W), nominalization (n), plural nominalization (X)
- Remove verb (V) and progressive/gerund (G)

But wait, `d` without `V` means `versed` would only be an adjective (which is correct - "well-versed"). And `S` without `V` means `verses` would only be a plural noun (which is correct for the legitimate use).

Also, we should remove `V` from `versus`:
Current: `versus/~PV`
Proposed: `versus/~P`

And check `versed`:
Current: `versed/~JVtTU`
This has:
- `~` common
- `J` adjective
- `V` verb
- `t` preterite (simple past) verb form
- `T` past participle verb form
- `U` un- prefix

Wait, `versed` with verb flags means it can be used as a verb. But "versed" as a verb form of "verse" (meaning "to compose verse") is legitimate but very rare. The more common use is "well-versed" (adjective). The PR specifically says: "versed" also passes spellcheck because it's also a different word, most commonly seen in the phrase "well-versed".

So `versed` as an adjective should stay, but the verb sense should probably be removed since it contributes to the false negative for the "verse" vs "versus" issue.

Current: `versed/~JVtTU` 
Proposed: `versed/~JTU` (remove `V` and `t`, keep `J`, `T`, `U`)

Wait, but `T` is past participle which is a verb form. Let me check: `T` is "verb past participle form property". And `t` is "verb preterite / simple past form property". These are verb forms, so they imply verb. If we remove `V`, should we also remove `t` and `T`?

Actually, looking at the annotations.json, these are properties, not affixes:
- `V` = "verb property" (base verb)
- `t` = "verb preterite / simple past form property"
- `T` = "verb past participle form property"
- `J` = "adjective property"

So `versed/~JVtTU` means: common word, adjective, verb, preterite, past participle, un- prefix.

If we remove `V` and `t`, we get `versed/~JTU`. But `T` (past participle) without `V` might be fine - many past participles can be used as adjectives.

Actually, let me reconsider. The PR description says:
- "versed" also passes spellcheck because it's also a different word, most commonly seen in the phrase "well-versed".

This seems to be saying that "versed" is fine as an adjective, and the issue is only when it's used as a verb meaning "competed against". But the PR doesn't explicitly say to change "versed".

Let me focus on the minimal changes that address the PR:

1. The main issue: "verse" used as a verb related to "versus". 
2. "versing" is archaic - remove from dictionary.
3. "verses" may be used as a verb form and as a misspelling for the preposition.

The simplest approach: modify the dictionary entries.

use harper_core::spell::FstDictionary;
use harper_core::spell::Dictionary;

fn main() {
    let dict = FstDictionary::curated();
    
    println!("=== Checking dictionary entries ===");
    
    // Check "verse"
    let verse_meta = dict.get_word_metadata_str("verse");
    println!("verse metadata: {:?}", verse_meta);
    println!("verse is verb: {:?}", verse_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_verb()));
    println!("verse is noun: {:?}", verse_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_noun()));
    
    // Check "verses"
    let verses_meta = dict.get_word_metadata_str("verses");
    println!("verses metadata: {:?}", verses_meta);
    println!("verses is verb: {:?}", verses_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_verb()));
    println!("verses is noun: {:?}", verses_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_noun()));
    
    // Check "versing"
    let versing_meta = dict.get_word_metadata_str("versing");
    println!("versing metadata: {:?}", versing_meta);
    println!("versing is verb: {:?}", versing_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_verb()));
    
    // Check "versed"
    let versed_meta = dict.get_word_metadata_str("versed");
    println!("versed metadata: {:?}", versed_meta);
    println!("versed is verb: {:?}", versed_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_verb()));
    println!("versed is adjective: {:?}", versed_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_adjective()));
    
    // Check "versus"
    let versus_meta = dict.get_word_metadata_str("versus");
    println!("versus metadata: {:?}", versus_meta);
    println!("versus is verb: {:?}", versus_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.is_verb()));
    println!("versus is preposition: {:?}", versus_meta.as_ref().map(|m| m.preposition));
    
    // Check if "versing" is in the dictionary
    println!("Contains 'versing': {}", dict.contains_word_str("versing"));
    println!("Contains 'versed': {}", dict.contains_word_str("versed"));
    println!("Contains 'verses': {}", dict.contains_word_str("verses"));
}

Let me check its current entry: `versed/~JVtTU`\n\n- `~` = common\n- `J` = adjective\n- `V` = verb\n- `t` = preterite (simple past)\n- `T` = past participle\n- `U` = un- prefix\n\nThe PR says "versed" is fine as an adjective ("well-versed"). But as a verb form, it contributes to the false negative. However, `versed` as a past tense of "verse" (to compose poetry) is legitimate. \n\nI think we should keep `versed` as is for now, since the PR doesn\'t explicitly say to change it and it has legitimate uses. The `d` suffix target is:\n```json\n"target": [\n    {\n        "metadata": {\n            "adjective": { "degree": "Positive" }\n        }\n    },\n    {\n        "if_base": {\n            "verb": {}\n        },\n        "metadata": {\n            "verb": { "verb_form": "PAST" }\n        }\n    }\n]\n```\n\nThe first target (adjective) always applies. The second target (verb past) only applies if the base is a verb. Since we removed `V` from `verse`, the base is no longer a verb, so `versed` generated from `verse` + `d` would only be an adjective. 2026-06-01 04:19:03,303 - INFO - swea-agent-Automattic__harper-2973 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now, what about `versed`? Let me check its current entry: `versed/~JVtTU`

- `~` = common
- `J` = adjective
- `V` = verb
- `t` = preterite (simple past)
- `T` = past participle
- `U` = un- prefix

The PR says "versed" is fine as an adjective ("well-versed"). But as a verb form, it contributes to the false negative. However, `versed` as a past tense of "verse" (to compose poetry) is legitimate. 

I think we should keep `versed` as is for now, since the PR doesn't explicitly say to change it and it has legitimate uses. The main changes are removing `V` and `G` from `verse` (so "verse" is no longer a verb lemma and "versing" is no longer generated), and removing `V` from `versus`.

But wait, I also need to check: what about the `d` suffix on `verse`? The `d` suffix generates `versed`. But `versed` already has its own dictionary entry. The expansion system would generate `versed` from `verse` + `d`, but since `versed` already exists as a separate entry, the metadata would be merged.

Let me check if removing `V` from `verse` affects the `d` expansion. The `d` suffix target is:
```json
"target": [
    {
        "metadata": {
            "adjective": { "degree": "Positive" }
        }
    },
    {
        "if_base": {
            "verb": {}
        },
        "metadata": {
            "verb": { "verb_form": "PAST" }
        }
    }
]
```

The first target (adjective) always applies. The second target (verb past) only applies if the base is a verb. Since we removed `V` from `verse`, the base is no longer a verb, so `versed` generated from `verse` + `d` would only be an adjective. But `versed` has its own entry with `V`, so when merged, the verb metadata from the separate entry would still apply.

Actually, I'm not sure how the merging works. Let me check if `versed` being generated from `verse+d` and also having its own entry causes issues.

Let me now also consider if we should add a new lint rule. Looking at the PR description examples:
- "We were having that discussion last night about train ride verse plane ride."
- "A game of Morra, with 3 different AI you can verse against."
- "This will make you switch to Smite and Fanatisim if you are versing a particular boss."

These examples show "verse" being used where "versus" would be correct. So another approach would be to add a phrase correction that maps "verse" to "versus" in certain contexts. But context-dependent rules are more complex.

For now, let me verify my dictionary changes are correct and then run the tests.

Now let me think about edge cases and whether we need additional changes:

1. **"verses"**: This is now only a plural noun (from `verse` + `S`). But the PR mentions "verses" can be a misspelling for "versus". For example: "That 10% could ruin that extension even though there might be 100k actually using it verses only 4k". In this case, "verses" should be "versus". This would require a phrase-level lint rule.

2. **"versed"**: Still has verb flags (`Vt`) in its own entry. Should we remove those? "Versed" as in "I versed him" is the misuse. But "versed" as in "well-versed" is fine. Since `versed` has its own entry `versed/~JVtTU`, it will still be recognized as a verb. But the PR says this is a different word ("well-versed"), so it's okay.

3. **"versing"**: Now removed from the dictionary (no longer generated since we removed `G`). This matches the PR: "versing is archaic. We can probably remove it from the dictionary."

4. **"versus"**: Now only a preposition (removed `V`). This is correct.

But wait, I need to check if "verses" as a plural of "verse" (poetry) still works. Yes, the `S` suffix is still present, so "verses" = plural of "verse" (noun) is still generated.

Now, the PR also mentions that "verses" may be used as a misspelling for "versus". Should I add a phrase correction for this? Let me check if there's an existing mechanism to handle this...

Actually, looking at the PR description again, it says:
> Suggested replacements could be: face, challenge, play against, compete against.

This suggests that the lint should suggest replacements when "verse" is used as a verb. But implementing a full context-dependent lint is complex. The dictionary changes I made are a good first step: they remove "verse" as a verb and "versing" from the dictionary.

However, I'm now thinking: maybe I should also add a phrase correction in the phrase_set_corrections module for common patterns like "verse against" → "versus" or "verse each other" etc.

Let me check if there's a simpler approach: maybe I should add a MapPhraseSetLinter entry for "verse" → "versus" in certain contexts.

Actually, let me re-examine the examples from the PR:
1. "train ride verse plane ride" - here "verse" = "versus"
2. "you can verse against" - "verse against" = "compete against"
3. "if you are versing a particular boss" - "versing" = "facing/fighting"
4. "you will verse a computer" - "verse" = "face/play against"
5. "I have versed both duos" - "versed" = "faced"
6. "never versed Torch" - "versed" = "faced"
7. "they want to verse each other" - "verse each other" = "face each other"
8. "you verse the computer" - "verse" = "face"
9. "I am versing the computer" - "versing" = "facing"
10. "The player is versing a PC" - same
11. "it verses the standard pipeline" - "verses" = "versus"
12. "in the U.S. verses those that are not" - "verses" = "versus"
13. "Balancing the amount of work on it verses other work" - "verses" = "versus"
14. "using it verses only 4k" - "verses" = "versus"
15. "Teams in Group1 does NOT verse teams in Group3" - "verse" = "face/play"
16. "Teams in Group1 verses every other team" - "verses" = "faces/plays"
17. "they verse other teams" - "verse" = "face/play"
18. "Teams in Group2 verse every other team once" - "verse" = "face/play"
19. "you can verse the bot online" - "verse" = "face/play"
20. "you can verse it" - same
21. "you can verse your current bot against an older version" - "verse against" = "pit against"
22. "you verse your friends" - "verse" = "face/play against"
23. "You will be versing the computer" - "versing" = "facing"
24. "the team they are versing" - "versing" = "facing"

So there are really two patterns:
A. "verse" used where "versus" (preposition) would be correct: "X verse Y" → "X versus Y"
B. "verse" used as a transitive verb meaning "compete against": "verse someone" → "face/challenge someone"

For pattern A, adding a phrase correction for "verses" → "versus" would help with examples 11-14.

But for the verb uses (which are now no longer recognized as verbs since we removed `V`), the spell checker will flag them as misspellings. Let me verify this.

Actually, with `V` removed from `verse`, the word "verse" itself is still in the dictionary as a noun. So "verse" won't be flagged by the spell checker - it will just be treated as a noun. The issue is that in contexts like "I will verse you", "verse" would be parsed as a noun, which might cause other grammar rules to fire (like "determiner without noun" etc.).

But the PR's main goal seems to be just flagging these uses. Since "versing" is now removed from the dictionary entirely, it WILL be flagged as a misspelling. And "verses" as a verb form won't be generated (only as plural noun).

Let me also check if we need to handle "vs." which is an abbreviation for "versus

1. `verse/~NgSVGdrWnX` → `verse/~NgSdrWnX` (removed `V` and `G`)
2. `versus/~PV` → `versus/~P` (removed `V`)

Let me check if the PR description implies that changes to `versed` are needed. The PR says:
- "versed" also passes spellcheck because it's also a different word, most commonly seen in the phrase "well-versed"

This is just explaining the current state. It doesn't say to change it. But the examples include "versed" used as a verb ("I have versed both duos", "Newer players never versed Torch").

If we want to flag "versed" as a verb, we'd need to remove the verb flags from `versed`. But "versed" as a past participle adjective ("well-versed") is fine, and "versed" as a past tense of "verse" (to compose poetry) is legitimate.

I think the dictionary changes I've made are the right first step. The removal of `V` from `verse` means:
- "verse" is no longer recognized as a verb lemma
- "verses" is no longer generated as a third-person singular verb
- "versing" is no longer generated at all

These changes address the core issue: "verse" and its inflections will no longer be accepted as verbs.

However, I'm now wondering if I should also add a phrase correction entry for the common error pattern. Let me look at how the `CompulseToCompel` example works - it maps the non-standard "compulse" to "compel" with all its inflections. Similarly, we could map "verse" (as verb) to "face" or "play against".

But the problem is that "verse" as a noun is very common and would generate many false positives. The `CompulseToCompel` mapping works because "compulse" is rarely used as a noun.

Let me check if there's a `MapPhraseSetLinter` that can work with context (word sequences). Looking at the existing examples:
- "DoesOrDose" maps "dose not" → "does not" etc. This uses multi-word patterns.
- "HomeInOn" maps "hone in on" → "home in on".

So we could potentially add patterns like "verse against" → "compete against" or "verses against" → "competes against". But this would require adding multiple patterns.

Now let me think about whether I need to add a lint rule for "verses" as a misspelling of "versus". Looking at the examples from the PR:
- "Is there any disadvantages to it versEs the standard pipeline" 
- "how many adoptable dogs are house trained in the U.S. versEs those that are not"
- "Balancing the amount of work on it versEs other work"
- "That 10% could ruin that extension even though there might be 100k actually using it versEs only 4k"

These are cases where "verses" (the plural noun) is used instead of "versus" (the preposition). This is a common error.

But adding a simple 1-to-1 mapping "verses" → "versus" would cause false positives for the legitimate plural noun use ("several verses of poetry").

However, looking at the existing phrase_set_corrections, many of them are aggressive (e.g., "compulse" → "compel" even though "compulse" is a legitimate noun). The philosophy seems to be that it's better to flag and let the user decide.

But I'm not sure if the PR expects this change. The PR description mentions these examples as illustrations of the problem, and my dictionary changes address the core issue (removing verb sense of "verse").

Let me also check if there should be a change to the `versed` entry. The PR examples include:
- "I have versed both duos and trios"
- "Newer players never versed Torch"

With my changes, "versed" still has its own verb entry (`versed/~JVtTU`). So "versed" as a verb would still be accepted. Should I remove the verb flags from `versed`?

Let me look at the tagged file again to see if there were changes related to "versed"... I already checked and there were none. So maybe the `versed` entry is fine as is.

But wait, if "verse" is no longer a verb lemma, then "versed" as a past tense verb form doesn't have a valid lemma. However, "versed" has its own standalone entry, so it's independent.

I think the minimal changes I've made are correct. Let me also consider if I should remove `V` and `t` from `versed`:

Current: `versed/~JVtTU`
- `J` = adjective (well-versed)
- `V` = verb
- `t` = preterite (simple past)
- `T` = past participle
- `U` = un- prefix

If I remove `V` and `t`, it becomes `versed/~JTU`:
- Still adjective
- Still past participle (can be used adjectivally)
- Still un- prefix (unversed)

This would make "versed" no longer a simple past verb. But "versed" as a past participle (in passive constructions like "was versed") would still work with `T`.

Actually, I think I should also remove `V` and `t` from `versed` to be consistent. Let me do that:

Let me now summarize the changes I've made:

1. **`verse/~NgSVGdrWnX` → `verse/~NgSdrWnX`**: Removed `V` (verb) and `G` (progressive/-ing suffix). This means:
   - "verse" is no longer recognized as a verb lemma
   - "versing" is no longer generated (removed from dictionary)
   - "verses" is no longer generated as a third-person singular verb (only as plural noun)

2. **`versus/~PV` → `versus/~P`**: Removed `V` (verb). "versus" is now only a preposition, which is correct.

3. **`versed/~JVtTU` → `versed/~JTU`**: Removed `V` (verb) and `t` (preterite/simple past). "versed" is now only an adjective (as in "well-versed") and past participle (which can be used adjectivally), not a simple past verb.

These changes ensure that:
- "verse" is no longer treated as a verb, so using it as one (meaning "to compete against") will be flagged
- "versing" is completely removed from the dictionary (it was archaic)
- "verses" is no longer a verb form (only plural noun)
- "versed" is no longer a simple past verb (only adjective/past participle)
- "versus" is only a preposition (not a verb)

These changes align with the PR description's requirements.</think>Let me verify the final state of the changes and run the full test suite one more time to ensure everything is clean
